# Creating Accessible PDFs | Digital Accessibility Services

> ## Excerpt
> PDFs are a versatile and convenient way to share information. But PDFs are often not structured properly, which can create barriers for people who need to read and understand the information.Fortunate

---
PDFs are a versatile and convenient way to share information. But PDFs are often not structured properly, which can create barriers for people who need to read and understand the information.

Fortunately, there are accessibility best practices that can help make PDFs more accessible for everyone.

If you need an accessible doc fast, consider using [Harvard's preferred document remediation vendor, AbleDocs](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/pdf-vendors).

**In this page:**

-   [Check if your PDF has tags](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/pdf#check)
-   [Review Reading Order and Tags](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/pdf#review)
-   [Ensure Image Accessibility](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/pdf#images)
-   [Set File Properties](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/pdf#properties)

___

## PDF Accessibility Best Practices

## PDF Accessibility Best Practices

-   **Start with an accessible source document:** Whether you start in Word, Google Docs, PowerPoint, or InDesign, we recommend making your source document as accessible as possible before converting to PDF. Review our [Creating Accessible Documents guidance](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/accessible-documents) to ensure you're following best practices for your document type.
-   **Keep accessibility settings when you convert the document:** When converting to PDF, use settings that retain tags and accessibility formatting. Avoid "Print to PDF" settings as they will remove your tags and accessibility formatting.
-   **Does it really need to be a PDF?** Consider if a PDF is really the best way to share the information. Would a different format be easier to make accessible and work better for the content? Before you create a PDF, here are a few considerations:
    -   If your PDF is a form: consider using a Google or Qualtrics form instead. This allows for streamlined data collection, autofill access, and better version control. A web form is also easier to create and much more accessible than a PDF.
    -   If your PDF is informational: consider sharing the content on a web page. It’s much easier to maintain and update web pages, and HTML is generally more accessible than PDFs. Your audience is also more likely to read a web page than to download and read a PDF, especially on mobile.

## Check if your PDF has tags

### Open the Tags Panel

Tags provide structure for content in your document.

![tags button.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/pdf_-_tags-button.png)

 To see if your PDF has tags, open the Tags Panel. Navigate to View > Show/Hide > Navigation Panes > Tags.

### Fix “No Tags Available”

If you don't see any tags, you may have to add them!

![Autotag document button.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/pdf-autotag-document.png)

In the Accessibility panel select "Autotag Document." The tags will not be perfect, but they will be a good start!

## What Tags you might see

### Heading Tags

Headings provide structure and organization to your document. Generally, there should only be one Heading 1 (<H1>) for your document. Headings should be used in ascending numerical order. Don’t skip heading levels!

![heading tag.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/pdf-_tags_-_h1.png)

Tags: <H1>, <H2>, <H3>, <H4>, <H5>, <H6>

### List Tags

Lists help organize content and should be tagged with List <L> for the entire lit, List Item <LI> for each item in the list, and List Body <LBody> for the content of each list item.

![Tag tree with <l>, </l></p><li>, <lbody> tags.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/pdf-tags-list.png)

Tags: <L>, <LI>, <LBody>

### Paragraph Tags

Most text in your document will be tagged with a <P> tag. This indicates regular paragraph text.

![Paragraph tag.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/pdf-tags-paragraph.png)

Tags: <P>

### Link Tags

All links should have [descriptive link text](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/write-helpful-links) and be tagged.

![Tag tree showing <link>, link-objr, and google.com.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/screen_shot_2022-11-14_at_11.37.30_am.png)

Tags: <Link> and Link-OBJR

## Review the Reading Order and Tags

### Reading Order

The order of the tags displayed in the Tags Panel is called the Reading Order.

Use your up and down arrow keys to navigate through the tags. As you do, content in your PDF will be highlighted. 

Make sure the order of the tags matches the order of content as you would read it visually.

![Tags panel showing h tags, p tags, and l tags.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/pdf_-_tags.png)

### How to move a tag in the reading order

If you need to move a tag you can right click on the tag and select “Cut” from the menu (or CMD/CTRL + X). After you cut the tag you can paste it where you like in the tag tree by right clicking the tag just before the correct location and select “Paste” (or CMD/CTRL + V). The cut tag will be placed next to the tag you highlighted.

### How to modify an existing tag

-   Right click on the tag you want to change
-   Go down to Properties
-   Object Properties box will pop up
-   “In the Tag tab, select the tag from the options in the ‘Type’ menu/list.

## Ensure image accessibility

![Set alternate text box for image 1 of 3.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/online-accessibility-huit/files/pdf-set-alt-text-2.png)

Image Tags: Images should be tagged as <figure> and they should have [useful alt text](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/describe-content-images "Write helpful Alt Text to describe images").

Adding Alt Text: In Accessibility Panel, select “Set Alternate Text.” A pop up will guide you through your images in the document. You can add alt or edit alt text, or select “Decorative” for unimportant images.

Tags: <Figure>

## Check Color Contrast

 ![color contrast icon.](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/sites/g/files/omnuum12446/files/styles/hwp_1_1__100x100_scale/public/online-accessibility-huit/files/icon_contrast_png_01.png?itok=g3isd9Vb)

Text and other elements in your PDF should have [sufficient color contrast](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/use-sufficient-color-contrast) against the background. Color Contrast should be 4.5:1 or greater for standard size text. If you are using Adobe's Accessibility Checker you will always be prompted to check the color contrast. If you're not sure about the contrast, use a tool (such as the [Color Contrast Analyser](https://www.tpgi.com/color-contrast-checker/)) to check.

If you find contrast errors at this stage it is always better to go back to the source document to correct the contrast when possible. This is why it is so important to start with an accessible source document.

## Set File Properties

### Add or Verify Metadata

-   Add Title and Author (File > Properties>Description Tab)
-   Set Doc Title to Display (Properties> Initial View tab >Window Options > Show > Document Title)
-   Add Language (Properties>Advanced Tab > Reading Options >Language)

### Be Mindful of Security Settings

If using, check the “Enable text access to screen reader devices for the visually impaired” check box in the “Permissions” section of the security settings.

This applies to security settings that restrict permissions to the document (i.e. copy and paste) not just adding a password.

## Resources

-   [Tagged PDF Best Practices (PDF Association)](https://www.pdfa.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/TaggedPDFBestPracticeGuideSyntax.pdf)
-   [Video: Testing a PDF for Accessibility (Section 508.gov)](https://www.section508.gov/training/pdfs/aed-cop-pdf02/)

## Yes. Here is the requirements inventory.

Strictly, a PDF is considered accessible when it satisfies the relevant accessibility standard in context — usually **WCAG** requirements as applied to PDFs, and often **PDF/UA** expectations for tagged PDF — **and** the result is actually usable with assistive technology. There is no honest “two-box checklist” that guarantees this, because image text and missing tags are obvious failures, but passing basic checks still does not guarantee full accessibility. fileciteturn3file9 fileciteturn4file0

## A PDF has to do all of the following

### 1. Contain real text, not just pictures of text

- The document cannot be only a scanned image of text.
- Users must be able to select/search the text.
- If the PDF started as scanned pages, OCR has to produce an accurate machine-readable text layer.
- The text has to be extractable by assistive technology, not just visually painted on the page. fileciteturn3file9 fileciteturn4file0

### 2. Map characters correctly to actual text

- Fonts and encoding must allow characters to be extracted correctly to Unicode.
- Screen readers, text export, Braille output, and copy/paste need the actual characters, not broken glyph mappings. fileciteturn4file0

### 3. Have a logical tag structure

- The PDF must be a **tagged PDF**, not an untagged visual layout.
- Tags must identify what content actually is: headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, figures, links, sections, and so on.
- If the PDF has **no tags**, it is not accessible. fileciteturn3file6 fileciteturn4file0

### 4. Have the correct reading order

- The tag order has to match the order the content should be read.
- Multi-column layouts, sidebars, headers/footers, captions, and floating visuals cannot scramble that order.
- Reading order must make sense for nonvisual linear reading, not just visual scanning. fileciteturn4file1 fileciteturn4file0

### 5. Preserve meaningful document structure

- Headings must be tagged as headings.
- Heading levels must be logical and not skipped arbitrarily.
- There should generally be one top-level heading for the document.
- Paragraphs must be paragraphs, not just loose text objects. fileciteturn4file1

### 6. Represent lists as real lists

- Lists must be tagged as lists.
- List items must be tagged as list items.
- List body/content must be represented properly.
- You cannot fake lists with visual bullets and hope assistive tech infers it. fileciteturn4file1

### 7. Represent links as real links

- Links must be tagged as links.
- Link text must be descriptive enough to make sense out of context.
- Link targets must be operable without a mouse. fileciteturn4file1 fileciteturn4file0

### 8. Represent tables as real tables

- Tables must be structurally identified as tables.
- Rows, cells, and headers must be represented semantically.
- Data tables cannot just be visually aligned text.
- Table reading order and header relationships must make sense to assistive technology. fileciteturn4file0

### 9. Give non-text content a text equivalent

- Informative images need useful alt text.
- Decorative images should be marked decorative/artifacted so they do not pollute reading order.
- Figures need figure semantics.
- Charts, diagrams, multimedia, and other non-text content need an equivalent that preserves the information they carry.
- Interactive form controls also need text equivalents such as names/tooltips where applicable. fileciteturn4file1 fileciteturn4file0

### 10. Support forms accessibly, if the PDF contains forms

- Form fields must be interactive.
- Each field must have a proper accessible name/label.
- Tab order must be logical.
- Instructions and completion tips must be available.
- Error prevention and error identification must be accessible.
- The form cannot depend on a mouse.
- Timing limits cannot block completion unless the user can request more time. fileciteturn3file16 fileciteturn4file0

### 11. Support navigation, if the document is long or interactive

- Internal navigation aids should exist where appropriate.
- Bookmarks should be present where useful.
- A table of contents, headings, and internal links should help users move through the file efficiently.
- Navigation must be keyboard accessible. fileciteturn4file0

### 12. Declare the document language

- The PDF needs a document language so screen readers can choose the right pronunciation/voice behavior.
- Language changes inside the document should also be handled where relevant. fileciteturn4file0 fileciteturn4file1

### 13. Expose a meaningful document title

- The file needs a proper title in metadata.
- The PDF should be configured to display the document title, not just the filename, where that setting applies.
- Users need to be able to identify what the document is. fileciteturn4file1 fileciteturn4file0

### 14. Keep security from blocking assistive technology

- Security settings cannot prevent screen readers from accessing the text.
- Restrictions on printing/copying/editing cannot destroy text access for assistive tech. fileciteturn4file1 fileciteturn4file0

### 15. Not rely on color alone

- Meaning cannot be communicated by color alone.
- Any color-coded distinction needs another cue such as text, pattern, label, or structure. fileciteturn4file0

### 16. Have sufficient color contrast

- Text and other relevant elements need sufficient contrast against the background.
- One of the docs you gave specifies **4.5:1 or greater for standard-size text**. fileciteturn4file1

### 17. Not rely only on sensory or positional cues

- Instructions cannot depend only on things like “click the red button,” “see the box on the right,” or other purely visual/sensory references without an accessible equivalent. fileciteturn4file0

### 18. Use text instead of images of text

- Important textual information should be real text whenever possible, not baked into an image. fileciteturn4file0

### 19. Avoid flashing/blinking hazards

- The PDF cannot include flashing or blinking content that creates accessibility or seizure-related problems. fileciteturn4file0

### 20. Avoid unexpected focus changes

- Focus should not jump unpredictably without user initiation.
- Interactive behavior should be stable and understandable. fileciteturn4file0

### 21. Keep navigation and identification consistent

- Repeated navigation mechanisms and repeated interface elements should behave consistently.
- Similar controls should be identified consistently. fileciteturn4file0

### 22. Reflow and resize in a usable way

- The document structure should support reflow/resizing so content remains usable at larger zoom levels and on smaller screens.
- Tagged structure is part of what makes this possible. fileciteturn3file10 fileciteturn4file0

### 23. Be operable with assistive technology and keyboard access

- Screen readers must be able to read it.
- Screen magnifiers must be able to work with it.
- Keyboard users must be able to operate interactive features.
- The PDF needs to work with common assistive technologies, not just look correct visually. fileciteturn4file0

### 24. Start from an accessible source and preserve accessibility on export

This is upstream, but it is still part of whether the PDF ends up accessible in practice.

- The source document has to be authored accessibly.
- Export must preserve tags and accessibility information.
- “Print to PDF” workflows that strip tags and structure are not acceptable.
- If source accessibility is broken, the PDF usually inherits the damage. fileciteturn3file1 fileciteturn4file1

## Conditional requirements people forget

These are still part of the “every single thing” answer, but only when the document contains that feature.

### If the PDF has multimedia

- Audio/video needs an equivalent.
- Audio needs controls. fileciteturn4file0

### If the PDF has forms

- Names, labels, instructions, errors, tab order, timing, keyboard access all matter. fileciteturn3file16 fileciteturn4file0

### If the PDF has complex graphics

- Alt text alone may be insufficient; the informational content has to be conveyed somehow. fileciteturn4file0

### If the PDF has long structure/navigation demands

- Headings, bookmarks, TOC, and internal links become much more important. fileciteturn4file0

## The two absolute fail-fast tests

A PDF is plainly inaccessible if either of these is true:

1. It is an image of text instead of real text.
2. It has no tags. fileciteturn3file9

That does **not** mean the reverse is sufficient. A tagged, searchable PDF can still be inaccessible because of bad reading order, garbage alt text, broken forms, bad tables, poor contrast, or other failures. fileciteturn3file9

## The shortest accurate version

For a PDF to be considered accessible, it must:

1. Have real machine-readable text.
2. Encode characters correctly.
3. Be properly tagged.
4. Have correct reading order.
5. Represent headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, links, and figures semantically.
6. Provide usable text alternatives for non-text content.
7. Support accessible forms when forms exist.
8. Support navigation and keyboard use.
9. Include document language and title metadata.
10. Avoid security settings that block assistive tech.
11. Meet contrast and non-color-only requirements.
12. Avoid flashing, unexpected focus changes, and inconsistent navigation.
13. Remain usable under zoom/reflow/assistive technology.
14. Actually work for disabled users, not just pass a superficial checker. fileciteturn4file0 fileciteturn4file1 fileciteturn3file9

**requirement**, **why it exists**, **PDF construct behind it**, and **how you would test it without Adobe**.

## Legend

- **A** = can **author** that requirement into a **new PDF**
- **R** = can **remediate/edit** that requirement in an **existing PDF**
- **I** = can **inspect/test/read** that requirement
- **T** = can help with the **text layer only** (OCR/searchability), not full semantics
- **V** = helps at the **viewer/UI layer**, not by fixing the PDF itself
- **M** = supports a **narrow slice** such as metadata or forms
- **—** = I did not find meaningful documented support

A warning before the matrix: some requirements are fundamentally **content-authoring and human-QA requirements**, not “library features.” No JavaScript tool can honestly guarantee things like “the alt text is actually useful” or “the reading order is truly correct” without human judgment. Your requirement docs are explicit that basic checks help, but they do not guarantee full accessibility. fileciteturn0file0 fileciteturn0file1 fileciteturn0file3 fileciteturn0file4

## Matrix 1: text, structure, semantics

| Requirement                                          | PDF.js | Scribe.js | PDFKit | pdf-lib   | Apryse    | Nutrient  |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ------ | --------- | ------ | --------- | --------- | --------- |
| Real searchable/selectable text                      | **I**  | **T / R** | **A**  | **I / M** | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| Correct character extraction / text layer inspection | **I**  | **T**     | **A**  | **I**     | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| Tagged PDF / structure tree exists                   | **I**  | —         | **A**  | —         | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| Correct logical reading order                        | **I**  | —         | **A**  | —         | **R / I** | **V**     |
| Headings and paragraph semantics                     | **I**  | —         | **A**  | —         | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| List semantics                                       | **I**  | —         | **A**  | —         | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| Table semantics                                      | **I**  | —         | **A**  | —         | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| Link semantics                                       | **I**  | —         | **A**  | **M**     | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| Figure semantics / alt text / artifacts              | **I**  | —         | **A**  | —         | **R / I** | **V / I** |
| OCR for image-only PDFs                              | —      | **T / R** | —      | —         | **R**     | —         |

**Why these scores:** PDF.js is strong at parsing and inspection, with APIs for text content, operator lists, structure trees, annotations, mark info, metadata, form objects, and permissions, but it is not a remediation authoring tool. Scribe.js is strong for OCR and searchable text layers. PDFKit is the strongest open-source JS option for authoring tagged/logically structured PDFs from scratch. pdf-lib is not documented as a tag-tree accessibility tool. Apryse is the strongest no-Adobe JS option I found for actual PDF accessibility remediation and PDF/UA conversion workflows. Nutrient is strongest on accessible rendering/viewing of already-tagged PDFs rather than tag remediation. citeturn320891search4turn871573view12turn871573view15turn344813view0turn344813view1turn344813view4turn871573view0turn871573view2turn871573view3turn871573view4turn524580search5turn524580search8turn344813view10turn344813view11turn344813view13turn344813view15

## Matrix 2: metadata, navigation, forms, permissions

| Requirement                                       | PDF.js | Scribe.js | PDFKit               | pdf-lib               | Apryse             | Nutrient        |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------ | --------- | -------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------ | --------------- |
| Document title metadata                           | **I**  | —         | **A**                | **A / M**             | **R / I**          | **V / I**       |
| Document language metadata                        | **I**  | —         | **A**                | **A / M**             | **R / I**          | **V / I**       |
| Bookmarks / outline / navigation aids             | **I**  | —         | **A**                | **Partial / fragile** | **R / I**          | **V / I**       |
| Interactive form fields exist                     | **I**  | —         | **A**                | **A / M**             | **R / I**          | **V / I**       |
| Form labels / names / field access                | **I**  | —         | **Partial A**        | **Partial M**         | **R / I**          | **V / I**       |
| Logical tab order / field navigation              | **I**  | —         | **Partial A**        | **Partial M**         | **R / I**          | **V / I**       |
| Security / permissions inspection                 | **I**  | —         | **Partial A**        | **Partial M**         | **R / I**          | **V / I**       |
| Preserve higher-level info during copy/merge/edit | —      | —         | **Generation-first** | **Weak**              | **Strongest here** | **Viewer only** |

**Why these scores:** PDF.js can inspect form objects, metadata, permissions, and related document info. PDFKit can author title, language, outlines, forms, and security-related options, but its own docs are clear that its logical-structure support currently covers marked content rather than everything else in the file. pdf-lib can set title, language, author, keywords, and work with AcroForms, but its own docs say `copy()` does not preserve all information such as acroforms and outlines. Apryse documents editing, COS-level access, accessible reading-order mode, and PDF/UA conversion. Nutrient documents accessible UI, keyboard behavior, and HTML rendering of tagged/PDF-UA content rather than deep remediation. citeturn320891search0turn320891search1turn344813view6turn344813view7turn344813view8turn344813view9turn871573view6turn344813view5turn871573view9turn871573view10turn871573view11turn524580search6turn344813view10turn344813view11turn344813view13turn344813view14turn344813view15

## Matrix 3: requirements that are mostly human/content QA

These are real accessibility requirements, but no JavaScript library honestly “solves” them on its own.

| Requirement                                                    | PDF.js                   | Scribe.js             | PDFKit                     | pdf-lib             | Apryse                             | Nutrient                                    |
| -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | --------------------- | -------------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Alt text is actually useful                                    | **I only**               | —                     | **A only**                 | —                   | **Partial R / I**                  | **V only**                                  |
| Reading order is genuinely correct for humans + screen readers | **I only**               | —                     | **A only**                 | —                   | **Partial R / I**                  | **V only**                                  |
| Color is not the only cue                                      | —                        | —                     | **Author responsibility**  | —                   | **Partial tooling**                | **Viewer/UI only**                          |
| Contrast is sufficient                                         | —                        | —                     | **Author responsibility**  | —                   | **Partial tooling**                | **Viewer/UI only**                          |
| Sensory/positional instructions are not the only instructions  | —                        | —                     | **Author responsibility**  | —                   | **Partial tooling**                | **Viewer/UI only**                          |
| Table headers/relationships are meaningful, not merely tagged  | **I only**               | —                     | **A only**                 | —                   | **Partial R / I**                  | **V only**                                  |
| Form instructions/errors/timing are actually usable            | **I only**               | —                     | **Partial A**              | **Partial M**       | **Partial R / I**                  | **V only**                                  |
| Actual conformance/real usability with assistive tech          | **Inspect/support only** | **Text-only support** | **Authoring support only** | **Partial support** | **Closest full-stack option here** | **Accessible consumption, not remediation** |

This is the part people usually try to hand-wave away, but your requirements docs do not. They explicitly separate obvious machine-detectable failures from the things that still require judgment and real testing. fileciteturn0file1 fileciteturn0file3

## What each tool is actually for

| Tool          | What it actually does best                                                                                                 |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **PDF.js**    | Inspect existing PDFs: text, tags, structure tree, annotations, forms, metadata, permissions, operator lists               |
| **Scribe.js** | OCR image PDFs and add an invisible/searchable text layer                                                                  |
| **PDFKit**    | Generate new accessibility-aware PDFs from structured data you control                                                     |
| **pdf-lib**   | Metadata, basic forms, basic document manipulation                                                                         |
| **Apryse**    | The strongest no-Adobe JS option I found for real remediation/editing/conversion work, including PDF/UA-oriented workflows |
| **Nutrient**  | Accessible viewer/UI and semantic HTML rendering of already-tagged PDFs                                                    |

That summary follows the official tool docs I checked. citeturn320891search7turn344813view0turn344813view1turn344813view4turn871573view0turn344813view7turn344813view8turn344813view9turn524580search5turn524580search8turn344813view13turn344813view15

## The blunt bottom line

If you want **open-source JavaScript only**, there is **no single tool** in this set that covers **every single accessibility requirement end to end** for arbitrary existing PDFs.

The closest realistic split is:

- **PDF.js** for audit/inspection
- **Scribe.js** for OCR/searchability
- **PDFKit** for authoring new accessible PDFs
- **pdf-lib** for metadata/forms/basic edits

If you want the closest **no-Adobe JavaScript remediation stack** for broken real-world PDFs, **Apryse** is the strongest documented option I found. Nutrient is strong for accessible viewing/rendering of tagged PDFs, but based on the docs I checked it is not the same thing as a full remediation/tag-edit stack. citeturn344813view10turn344813view11turn344813view13turn344813view15turn524580search5turn524580search8

I left **MuhammaraJS** out of the matrix because I could find documentation for low-level PDF manipulation, but not enough first-party accessibility coverage to score it honestly.

The requirement rows come from the accessibility requirements in the PDFs you uploaded: searchable text, tags, reading order, semantic structure, alt text, metadata/language, forms, navigation, security, contrast/non-color-only meaning, and the fact that automated checks do not replace human judgment. fileciteturn5file1 fileciteturn5file5 fileciteturn5file8 fileciteturn5file12 fileciteturn5file14 fileciteturn5file17

| **OCR image-only PDFs into searchable text**                                                                                  | Cannot do this                                                                     | Can OCR and add searchable text layer     | Cannot do this                                           | Cannot do this                                             | I did not verify first-party OCR docs today                                                                                       | I did not verify first-party OCR docs today                                     |
| **Extract text from existing PDFs**                                                                                           | Can extract text                                                                   | Can extract text                          | Cannot inspect existing files                            | Can partly work with text, but not as a parsing-first tool | Can inspect and edit page content                                                                                                 | Viewer-focused, not a parsing-first tool                                        |
| **Inspect tags / structure tree in existing PDFs**                                                                            | Can inspect                                                                        | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Cannot inspect existing files                            | I did not verify meaningful support                        | Can partly inspect through tagged reading-order features; deeper tag inspection was not clearly documented in the pages I checked | Can expose tagged structure through accessible HTML rendering                   |

| **Edit tags / logical structure in an existing PDF**                                                                          | Cannot remediate                                                                   | Cannot remediate                          | Generation-first only; not for existing-file remediation | I did not verify meaningful support                        | I did not verify first-party JS docs for full tag-tree remediation today                                                          | Cannot remediate                                                                |
| **Support correct reading order in a new PDF**                                                                                | Cannot author                                                                      | Cannot author                             | Can create in new PDFs                                   | Cannot meaningfully do this                                | Can edit content, but I did not verify first-party JS docs for reading-order authoring/remediation specifically                   | Cannot author                                                                   |
| **Inspect reading order in an existing PDF**                                                                                  | Can inspect structure-related information                                          | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Cannot inspect existing files                            | I did not verify meaningful support                        | Viewer can expose tagged reading order                                                                                            | Viewer can expose tagged reading order when tags already exist                  |
| **Create headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, figures, links semantically in new PDFs**                                       | Cannot author                                                                      | Cannot author                             | Can create in new PDFs                                   | I did not verify meaningful support                        | Can edit content at low level, but semantic authoring at this level was not clearly documented in the pages I checked             | Cannot author                                                                   |
| **Add alt text / actual text / artifacts in new PDFs**                                                                        | Cannot author                                                                      | Cannot author                             | Can create in new PDFs                                   | I did not verify meaningful support                        | I did not verify first-party JS docs for this exact capability today                                                              | Cannot author                                                                   |
| **Edit alt text / semantic non-text handling in existing PDFs**                                                               | Cannot remediate                                                                   | Cannot remediate                          | Cannot remediate existing files                          | I did not verify meaningful support                        | I did not verify first-party JS docs for this exact capability today                                                              | Cannot remediate                                                                |
| **Set document title**                                                                                                        | Can inspect                                                                        | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Can create in new PDFs                                   | Can set metadata                                           | Can edit metadata                                                                                                                 | Viewer can expose document content, but this is not its core job                |
| **Set document language**                                                                                                     | Can inspect                                                                        | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Can create in new PDFs                                   | Can set metadata                                           | Can edit metadata                                                                                                                 | Viewer can expose document content, but this is not its core job                |
| **Create bookmarks / outline navigation**                                                                                     | Cannot author                                                                      | Cannot author                             | Can create in new PDFs                                   | Can partly do this, but preservation is fragile            | Can edit bookmarks                                                                                                                | Viewer can use navigation, but not author it                                    |
| **Preserve outlines / acroforms during document copy or merge**                                                               | Not its job                                                                        | Not its job                               | Generation-first only                                    | Does not fully preserve all information during copy        | Can edit existing PDFs at low level                                                                                               | Not its job                                                                     |
| **Create form fields**                                                                                                        | Cannot author                                                                      | Cannot author                             | Can create form fields                                   | Can work with AcroForms                                    | Can create and edit PDF content and annotations                                                                                   | Viewer/editor product, but this is not the core accessibility claim I verified  |
| **Fill or manipulate existing forms**                                                                                         | Can inspect form objects                                                           | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Can create forms, not primarily inspect existing ones    | Can work with AcroForms                                    | Can edit annotations and PDF content                                                                                              | Viewer/editor product can handle forms, but I did not verify details here today |
| **Fully remediate accessible form labeling, tab order, and error behavior in existing PDFs**                                  | Cannot remediate                                                                   | Cannot remediate                          | Not the right tool                                       | Not the right tool                                         | I did not verify first-party JS docs that clearly cover full form-accessibility remediation today                                 | I did not verify this today                                                     |
| **Inspect permissions / security flags**                                                                                      | Can inspect                                                                        | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Can create some document settings                        | Can partly manipulate metadata-level document state        | Can inspect and edit documents                                                                                                    | Viewer-focused                                                                  |
| **Provide an accessible reading experience without fixing the PDF file itself**                                               | Can do this in a viewer context                                                    | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Not a viewer                                             | Not a viewer                                               | Can do this in WebViewer                                                                                                          | Can do this strongly in viewer/HTML rendering                                   |
| **Render tagged PDFs as accessible DOM / HTML for screen readers**                                                            | Viewer can expose content, but this is not its main documented accessibility claim | Cannot do this meaningfully               | Not a viewer                                             | Not a viewer                                               | Can insert tagged structure into the DOM in accessible reading-order mode                                                         | Can render tagged/PDF/UA content as WCAG-compliant HTML                         |
| **Guarantee useful alt text, correct reading order, good contrast, non-color-only meaning, and overall real-world usability** | Cannot guarantee; human QA still required                                          | Cannot guarantee; human QA still required | Cannot guarantee; human QA still required                | Cannot guarantee; human QA still required                  | Cannot guarantee; human QA still required                                                                                         | Cannot guarantee; human QA still required                                       |

## What each tool is actually good for

| Tool          | What it is actually good for                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| ------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **PDF.js**    | Inspecting existing PDFs: text, operator lists, annotations, structure trees, metadata, permissions, and form-related objects. citeturn114394view0turn114394view1                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| **Scribe.js** | OCR and searchable text layers for image PDFs; extracting existing text; inserting an invisible text layer. citeturn458782view9turn458782view10                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **PDFKit**    | Creating new accessibility-aware PDFs from structured source data you control, including Tagged PDF/PDF/UA-oriented authoring, logical structure, artifacts, title/language, outlines, and forms. Its documented limit is that logical structure currently supports marked content, not annotations/forms/other objects. citeturn458782view0turn458782view1turn458782view2turn458782view4turn458782view5 |
| **pdf-lib**   | Metadata, title/language, and AcroForm work. It is not a strong documented accessibility-remediation tool, and its own docs warn that `copy()` does not preserve everything such as acroforms and outlines. citeturn458782view6turn458782view7turn458782view8                                                                                                                                              |
| **Apryse**    | Broad PDF editing, page-content editing, COS-level access, bookmark editing, and an accessible reading-order mode in the web viewer for correctly tagged PDFs. I did **not** verify first-party JS docs today that clearly prove full tag-tree remediation or full PDF/UA remediation in JavaScript. citeturn492000search3turn492000search4turn492000search7                                               |
| **Nutrient**  | Accessible viewing/rendering: tagged PDF and PDF/UA rendered as WCAG 2.1-accessible HTML for screen readers. I did **not** verify deeper PDF-tag remediation claims today. citeturn458782view14                                                                                                                                                                                                              |

## The blunt bottom line

If you want **open-source JavaScript only**, the most realistic split is:

- **PDF.js** for inspection
- **Scribe.js** for OCR and searchable text
- **PDFKit** for creating new accessible PDFs
- **pdf-lib** for metadata and some forms work

That stack covers **pieces** of the requirement set, not the whole remediation problem. citeturn114394view0turn114394view1turn458782view9turn458782view10turn458782view0turn458782view1turn458782view2turn458782view6turn458782view7turn458782view8

If you want **no-Adobe JavaScript with the strongest current remediation/editing story**, **Apryse** is the closest thing I could substantiate today, but I am **not** claiming I verified every semantic-tag remediation feature in first-party JS docs. What I could verify is strong viewer accessibility plus broad low-level editing. citeturn492000search3turn492000search4turn492000search7

- **Can partly do this**
- **Can only inspect this**
- **Cannot really do this**

## Open-source JavaScript only

Using your requirement set, the open-source JS tools worth scoring are:

- **PDF.js** for parsing, inspection, and viewing. Its docs expose page/document inspection APIs like `getTextContent()`, `getOperatorList()`, `getStructTree()`, `getMetadata()`, `getPermissions()`, and `getFieldObjects()`. citeturn738597search2turn218440view0turn656357view0turn656357view1turn656357view2
- **Scribe.js** for OCR and searchable text layers. Its README says it can extract text from text-native PDFs, OCR image-native PDFs, and write PDFs with an invisible text layer, including inserting text into an existing PDF to make it searchable. citeturn218440view4turn656357view6
- **PDFKit** for generating new accessibility-aware PDFs. Its accessibility docs explicitly cover Tagged PDF / PDF/UA-oriented generation, title, language, logical structure, alternative text, artifacts, and natural reading order; it also documents outlines and forms. Its own docs also state a hard limit: it currently supports only **marked content** in the logical structure, not annotations, forms, or other objects. citeturn682458search4turn656357view5turn738597search0turn738597search1
- **pdf-lib** for metadata and AcroForms. Its docs expose low-level `catalog` and `context`, plus `setTitle`, `setLanguage`, and `getForm()`. Its own docs also warn that `copy()` does **not** copy all information, including acroforms and outlines. citeturn925976search1turn925976search3turn656357view3turn656357view4
- **MuhammaraJS** for low-level Node-side PDF creation/parsing/manipulation. The docs I found describe it as a maintained drop-in replacement for discontinued HummusJS and as a fast module for creating, parsing, and manipulating PDF files and streams. I did **not** find first-party accessibility/tagging/PDF/UA docs for it, so I score it conservatively. citeturn738597search3turn738597search4turn738597search5

Your uploaded requirement docs are clear that accessible PDFs need more than “has text” or “has tags”: they need real text, tags, correct reading order, semantic structure, useful alternatives for non-text, accessible forms/navigation/metadata, and human review beyond automated checks. fileciteturn0file0 fileciteturn0file1 fileciteturn0file2 fileciteturn0file3 fileciteturn0file4

## Tool × requirements matrix

| Requirement                                                            | PDF.js                                           | Scribe.js                                                        | PDFKit                                                           | pdf-lib                                                                      | MuhammaraJS                                                                                        |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Real searchable text in existing PDF                                   | **Can inspect whether it exists and extract it** | **Can create it for image PDFs by OCR and invisible text layer** | **Can create real text in new PDFs**                             | **Can work with PDFs, but this is not its main inspection strength**         | **Can manipulate PDFs at low level, but I did not verify text-layer accessibility features**       |
| OCR for image-only PDFs                                                | **Cannot do OCR**                                | **Can do OCR**                                                   | **Cannot do OCR**                                                | **Cannot do OCR**                                                            | **I did not verify OCR support**                                                                   |
| Character extraction / text-layer inspection                           | **Can inspect text content**                     | **Can extract or recognize text**                                | **Can author text in new PDFs**                                  | **Can partly work with document internals, but not as a parsing-first tool** | **Can parse/manipulate PDFs, but I did not verify Unicode/text-extraction accessibility features** |
| Tags / structure tree in existing PDFs                                 | **Can inspect structure tree when present**      | **Cannot meaningfully do this**                                  | **Cannot inspect existing PDFs as its main job**                 | **I did not verify meaningful tag-tree support**                             | **I did not verify meaningful tag-tree support**                                                   |
| Create tagged / structured PDF from scratch                            | **Cannot author PDFs this way**                  | **Cannot do this**                                               | **Can do this**                                                  | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Edit tags / structure in existing PDFs                                 | **Cannot remediate**                             | **Cannot remediate**                                             | **Not designed for existing-file remediation**                   | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Reading order in new PDFs                                              | **Cannot author**                                | **Cannot author**                                                | **Can author natural reading order and logical structure**       | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Inspect reading order in existing PDFs                                 | **Can inspect structure-related info**           | **Cannot do this**                                               | **Cannot inspect existing PDFs as its main job**                 | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Semantic headings / paragraphs / lists in new PDFs                     | **Cannot author**                                | **Cannot author**                                                | **Can author**                                                   | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Semantic tables in new PDFs                                            | **Cannot author**                                | **Cannot author**                                                | **Can author**                                                   | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Semantic figures / alt text / artifacts in new PDFs                    | **Cannot author**                                | **Cannot author**                                                | **Can author**                                                   | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Edit alt text / artifacts in existing PDFs                             | **Cannot remediate**                             | **Cannot remediate**                                             | **Not designed for existing-file remediation**                   | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                      | **I did not verify meaningful support**                                                            |
| Document title metadata                                                | **Can inspect**                                  | **Not its job**                                                  | **Can author**                                                   | **Can set**                                                                  | **I did not verify this specifically**                                                             |
| Document language metadata                                             | **Can inspect**                                  | **Not its job**                                                  | **Can author**                                                   | **Can set**                                                                  | **I did not verify this specifically**                                                             |
| Bookmarks / outlines in new PDFs                                       | **Can inspect related document info**            | **Not its job**                                                  | **Can author**                                                   | **Can be fragile during copying; not a strength**                            | **I did not verify this specifically**                                                             |
| Forms in new PDFs                                                      | **Cannot author**                                | **Cannot author**                                                | **Can create forms**                                             | **Can create and edit AcroForms**                                            | **Likely possible at low level, but I did not verify it from current docs**                        |
| Inspect form objects in existing PDFs                                  | **Can inspect field objects**                    | **Not its job**                                                  | **Not its main job**                                             | **Can work with AcroForms**                                                  | **Likely possible at low level, but I did not verify it from current docs**                        |
| Accessible form labeling / tab order / error behavior in existing PDFs | **Cannot remediate**                             | **Cannot remediate**                                             | **Limited, and logical structure excludes forms**                | **Not a full accessibility-remediation tool**                                | **I did not verify this specifically**                                                             |
| Permissions / security inspection                                      | **Can inspect permissions**                      | **Not its job**                                                  | **Can set some document options, but viewer enforcement varies** | **Can partly work with document state**                                      | **I did not verify this specifically**                                                             |
| End-to-end remediation of arbitrary broken PDFs                        | **No**                                           | **No**                                                           | **No**                                                           | **No**                                                                       | **Not proven from the docs I found**                                                               |
| Replace human accessibility judgment                                   | **No**                                           | **No**                                                           | **No**                                                           | **No**                                                                       | **No**                                                                                             |

## What that matrix actually means

### Best open-source tool for auditing existing PDFs

**PDF.js**\
It is the strongest open-source JS choice for inspecting text, structure tree, form objects, metadata, permissions, and page operators. citeturn218440view0turn656357view0turn656357view1turn656357view2

### Best open-source tool for OCR and searchable text

**Scribe.js**\
It is the clearest open-source JS answer for “this scanned PDF needs a searchable text layer.” citeturn218440view4turn656357view6

### Best open-source tool for creating a new accessible PDF

**PDFKit**\
It is the strongest open-source JS option with explicit accessibility docs: tagged output, title, language, logical structure, alt/actual text, artifacts, and reading order. But its own docs also say its logical-structure support does **not** currently cover annotations or forms. citeturn656357view5turn738597search0turn738597search1

### Best open-source tool for metadata and basic forms

**pdf-lib**\
It is useful for title/language metadata and AcroForms, but it is not a full accessibility-remediation tool, and its own docs warn that copying a document drops things like acroforms and outlines. citeturn925976search1turn925976search3turn656357view3turn656357view4

### Low-level wild card

**MuhammaraJS**\
It may be useful if you want Node-side low-level PDF plumbing, but I could not honestly score it as an accessibility tool because I did not find current first-party docs covering tagged-PDF or PDF/UA workflows. citeturn738597search3turn738597search5

## The blunt conclusion

If you mean **open-source JavaScript only**, there is **not** a single tool here that covers the full accessibility requirement set for arbitrary existing PDFs. The realistic split is:

- **PDF.js** for inspection
- **Scribe.js** for OCR/searchability
- **PDFKit** for creating new accessibility-aware PDFs
- **pdf-lib** for metadata and some forms work

That stack covers **pieces** of the requirement list, not the whole remediation problem. Your requirement docs are consistent with that reality: full PDF accessibility is multi-layered and still needs human review. fileciteturn0file0 fileciteturn0file1 fileciteturn0file2 fileciteturn0file3 fileciteturn0file4

I can do the same matrix next for **open-source Python only**, which is a much more interesting field.

## Open-source JavaScript only: tagging matrix

| Tool             | Can inspect whether tagging/structure exists                         | Can create tagged structure in **new** PDFs                        | Can edit/remediate tags in **existing** PDFs                          | What I verified it can tag or structure                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | Hard limits I verified                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Evidence                                                                                                                     |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **PDF.js**       | **Yes**                                                              | **No documented support**                                          | **No documented support**                                             | It can inspect page structure trees with `getStructTree()`, inspect `MarkInfo` with `getMarkInfo()`, and inspect related document data like metadata, permissions, and form field objects.                                                                                                                                                                              | I found **inspection APIs**, but no first-party tag-authoring or tag-remediation APIs in the docs I checked.                                                                                                                                                                                      | citeturn704162search0turn704162search1turn146741view0turn146741view1turn146741view2turn146741view3turn915311search1 |
| **PDFKit**       | **No, not as an existing-file inspector**                            | **Yes**                                                            | **No, not as an existing-file remediation tool**                      | It documents Tagged PDF / PDF/UA-oriented authoring: `tagged: true`, `subset: 'PDF/UA'`, title, language, logical structure, alt/actual text, artifacts, and natural reading order. It documents structure/tag types for headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, links, figures, and form widgets, and it supports automatic structure creation for `text()` and `list()`. | Its docs explicitly say it only supports **marked content** in the logical structure, **not annotations, forms, or anything else**. It also says it does **not** support Tagged PDF custom types mapped to standard types.                                                                        | citeturn675460view0                                                                                                       |
| **pdf-lib**      | **No meaningful first-party tag inspection API that I could verify** | **No first-party documented tag-creation API that I could verify** | **No first-party documented tag-remediation API that I could verify** | What is clearly documented is metadata (`setTitle`, `setLanguage`) and AcroForms (`PDFForm`).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | Its own docs say `copy()` does **not** copy all information, including **acroforms** and **outlines**. There is also a long-standing issue showing **links can be lost after combining PDFs**. That is bad news for preservation of accessibility-related structure/navigation in edit workflows. | citeturn675460view1turn964984view0turn964984view2turn964984view4turn707185search2turn276720search3                   |
| **Scribe.js**    | **No**                                                               | **No**                                                             | **No**                                                                | It can extract text from text-native PDFs, OCR image-native PDFs, and add an invisible text layer back into a PDF to make it searchable.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | That is **text-layer work**, not semantic tagging or structure-tree remediation.                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | citeturn147808search0                                                                                                     |
| **Tesseract.js** | **No**                                                               | **No**                                                             | **No**                                                                | It is an OCR engine for images.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | Its own docs say it **does not support PDF files** directly; they point people to **Scribe.js** or to rendering PDFs to images first.                                                                                                                                                             | citeturn907690search0turn907690search1                                                                                   |
| **MuhammaraJS**  | **No verified first-party tag/structure inspection support found**   | **No verified first-party tag/structure authoring support found**  | **No verified first-party tag/structure remediation support found**   | What I could verify is that it presents itself as a fast Node module for **creating, parsing, and manipulating PDF files and streams**.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | In the docs I found, I did **not** find first-party Tagged PDF, PDF/UA, structure-tree, alt-text, artifact, or reading-order APIs/documentation.                                                                                                                                                  | citeturn334763search0                                                                                                     |

## What this means, exactly

If the question is **“Which open-source JavaScript tool can actually do tagging?”**, the checked answer is:

- **PDFKit** is the only open-source JS tool in this set where I could verify **real tagged-structure authoring** from first-party docs. It can build a structure tree, assign standard structure element types, mark artifacts, set title/language, and carry alt/actual text. But it is mainly for **new PDFs you generate yourself**, and its own docs explicitly limit logical structure support to **marked content**, not annotations/forms/other objects. citeturn675460view0
- **PDF.js** can **inspect** tagging-related signals and structure, but I did not find first-party docs showing it as a tag authoring or remediation tool. citeturn704162search0turn704162search1turn146741view1turn146741view3
- **pdf-lib**, **Scribe.js**, **Tesseract.js**, and **MuhammaraJS** are **not verified tagging tools** based on the docs I checked. They each do other things, but not documented, end-to-end semantic tagging/remediation. citeturn675460view1turn147808search0turn907690search0turn334763search0

## The blunt conclusion

For **open-source JavaScript only**, I could verify exactly one serious tagging authoring option:

- **Create tagged PDFs from scratch:** **PDFKit**
- **Inspect whether tags/structure exist:** **PDF.js**
- **Repair the tag tree of arbitrary existing PDFs:** I did **not** find a verified open-source JavaScript tool in this set that clearly documents that capability

That is the cleaned-up answer.

If you want, I’ll do the same kind of **carefully checked tagging matrix for open-source Python**, which is where the landscape gets more interesting.

Yes — **for creating a new, mostly static accessible PDF from structured content you control, PDFKit can cover a large share of the authoring-side requirements**. Its own accessibility page explicitly says its checklist covers what is required to create a conformant **PDF/UA** document, including `tagged: true`, `subset: 'PDF/UA'`, title, language, logical structure, alternative/actual text, artifacts, natural reading order, and avoiding color-only meaning or flashing content. citeturn203788view0

But the honest answer is **not simply “yes, it does everything.”** The biggest limits are:

- **It is strongest for new PDF generation, not remediation of existing PDFs.** The docs describe authoring structure and marked content while you build the file. citeturn203788view0
- **Its logical-structure support has an explicit gap:** “So far, PDFKit only supports marked content in the logical structure, not annotations, forms, or anything else.” citeturn203788view0
- **Forms exist, but that is not the same as fully accessible structured forms.** PDFKit documents `formText`, `formPushButton`, `formCombo`, and `formList`, plus required/readOnly/default values and formatting options, so it can create forms. But because the accessibility docs explicitly exclude forms and annotations from logical structure support, I would not treat PDFKit alone as a complete answer for fully accessible PDF forms. citeturn343706view0turn343706view1turn203788view0
- **Bookmarks/navigation are supported.** PDFKit documents outlines/bookmarks via `addItem()`. citeturn343706view2
- **Accessibility still depends on content quality and human judgment.** Your requirement docs are clear that accessible PDFs need real text, tags, correct reading order, useful alternatives for non-text, proper metadata/language, and more — and that passing basic checks does not guarantee full accessibility. fileciteturn0file1 fileciteturn0file2 fileciteturn0file3 fileciteturn0file4

## The practical answer

### PDFKit is a strong fit when you are creating:

- reports
- handouts
- manuals
- guides
- other **new, structured, mostly static PDFs**

It can author:

- tagged structure
- reading-order-aware content
- headings/paragraphs/lists/tables/figures/links in the structure tree
- title and language
- alt/actual text
- artifacts
- bookmarks/outlines citeturn203788view0turn343706view2

### PDFKit is not a full answer when you need:

- remediation of arbitrary existing PDFs
- rich accessibility work on **forms/annotations inside the logical structure**
- a guarantee that the final file is truly accessible without manual review/testing citeturn203788view0 fileciteturn0file3

## My blunt conclusion

For **creating a new accessible PDF**, **PDFKit can do most of the heavy lifting for static document authoring**. For **fully accessible PDF forms** or **existing-file remediation**, **no, not by itself**. citeturn203788view0turn343706view0turn343706view1

If you want, I can turn that into a **PDFKit requirements checklist** with two columns: **“PDFKit can cover this”** and **“you still need another step/human review for this.”**

For the specific things you care about in accessibility work, PDF.js exposes first-party APIs to extract or inspect:

- **text content** with `getTextContent()` citeturn411027view1
- **page operator lists** with `getOperatorList()` citeturn411027view1
- **page structure trees** with `getStructTree()` citeturn411027view1
- **annotations** with `getAnnotations()` citeturn411027view1

- **metadata** with `getMetadata()` citeturn316654view1
- **AcroForm field data** with `getFieldObjects()` citeturn316654view2
- **permissions** with `getPermissions()` citeturn316654view0
- **outline/bookmarks** with `getOutline()` citeturn316654view5

So if the job is **“tell me what this existing PDF contains, structurally and semantically”**, PDF.js is the best mainstream open-source JS answer I could verify. citeturn411027view1turn316654view1turn316654view2turn316654view3turn316654view5

The main caveat is that PDF.js is strongest as an **inspector/parser/viewer**, not as a full authoring or remediation tool. Its docs say the **core layer** is advanced and not documented for stable direct use, while the documented API is the display layer. citeturn411027view0

Compared with the other open-source JS tools:

- **PDFKit** is much stronger for **creating new tagged PDFs**, not for inspecting arbitrary existing ones. citeturn411027view4
- **pdf-lib** is stronger for **editing/metadata/forms**, but I did not verify anything comparable to PDF.js’s first-party inspection surface for structure trees, MarkInfo, permissions, and operator lists. citeturn411027view3turn411027view1turn316654view0turn316654view3
- **Scribe.js** is for **OCR and text extraction/searchability**, not deep semantic inspection. citeturn411027view5

So the blunt version is:

- **Best open-source JS for extracting existing-PDF accessibility info:** **PDF.js**
- **Best open-source JS for creating new tagged PDFs:** **PDFKit**
- **Best open-source JS for OCR/searchable text layer:** **Scribe.js** citeturn411027view1turn411027view4turn411027view5

So structure extraction is page-oriented, not a neat whole-document semantic dump. citeturn920490view1turn920490view2                                 |

So if by “extract everything” you mean a stable public API over raw low-level internals, PDF.js does not present that as the supported path. citeturn920490view0                                                    |
| **Exact text-stream fidelity**                                  | `getTextContent()` is useful, but the docs note that **all occurrences of whitespace are replaced by standard spaces**. So it is an extraction API, not a perfect byte-faithful representation of the original text operators. citeturn920490view1                                                                                                                                                            |
| **Human-meaning judgments**                                     | PDF.js can tell you a lot about what is present, but it does not solve questions like “is the reading order actually right for humans?” or “is the alt text actually useful?” Your uploaded accessibility guidance is explicit that automated checks do not reliably judge reading order quality or alt-text usefulness, so extraction alone is not the whole story. fileciteturn0file1 fileciteturn0file3 |

So the clean version is:

- **Best open-source JS for extracting existing-PDF structure/info:** **PDF.js**. citeturn920490view0turn920490view1turn475777view0turn475777view1turn475777view2turn475777view3turn475777view4
