#### ChatGPT:
## PDF.js extraction checklist

Yes. If the job is **“what can I learn about an existing PDF?”**, this is the practical API map.

## Document-level facts

### Is the document tagged at all?

- `pdfDocument.getMarkInfo()`
- This returns the document’s **MarkInfo flags**, or `null` if no MarkInfo values are present. That is one of the first signals to check when you want to know whether the file is set up as a tagged PDF. citeturn367400view1

### What metadata does it expose?

- `pdfDocument.getMetadata()`
- This gives you both the **Info dictionary** and the **metadata section**. Use it for things like title/author/producer and other metadata payloads. citeturn367400view0

### What bookmarks / outline does it have?

- `pdfDocument.getOutline()`
- This returns the document’s outline tree if one exists. That is the API to use for bookmarks / navigation structure. citeturn367400view2

### What permissions does the PDF declare?

- `pdfDocument.getPermissions()`
- This returns the document’s permission flags, or `null` if none are present. citeturn367400view3

### Does it have form fields?

- `pdfDocument.getFieldObjects()`
- This returns `/AcroForm` field data for the JS sandbox, or `null` if the PDF has no field data. citeturn367400view4

### Does it contain document-level JavaScript?

- `pdfDocument.getJSActions()`
- This returns JavaScript actions from the name tree and from `A` or `AA` entries in the catalog dictionary, or `null` if none exist. That is useful for understanding interactive behavior and riskier PDFs. citeturn367400view5

### Does it have an open action?

- `pdfDocument.getOpenAction()`
- This tells you whether the file has a document open action, which can matter for viewer behavior and automation. citeturn367400view0

### Does it declare viewer preferences?

- `pdfDocument.getViewerPreferences()`
- This returns viewer preferences if present. citeturn367400view3

### What page mode / page layout does it request?

- `pdfDocument.getPageMode()`
- `pdfDocument.getPageLayout()`
- These expose the document’s requested page mode and layout. citeturn367400view3

### How big is the file payload?

- `pdfDocument.getDownloadInfo()`
- This resolves with the loaded PDF size in bytes. Useful for audits and pipelines, not accessibility by itself. citeturn367400view4

## Page-level facts

### What text is on the page?

- `pdfPage.getTextContent()`
- This returns the page’s text content. This is your main extraction call for the text layer on an existing PDF. The important caveat from the docs is that **all whitespace is normalized to standard spaces**, so this is extraction-oriented, not exact original text-stream fidelity. citeturn964825view1

### Can I stream text instead of loading it all at once?

- `pdfPage.streamTextContent()`
- This is the streaming version of text extraction. It has the same whitespace note. citeturn964825view1

### Does this page have a structure tree?

- `pdfPage.getStructTree()`
- This returns the page’s **structure tree**, or `null` if the current page has no structure tree. This is the key API for inspecting page-level tags/semantic structure in PDF.js. citeturn964825view1

### What annotations are on the page?

- `pdfPage.getAnnotations()`
- This returns the page’s annotation objects. Use it to inspect links, widgets, comments, and other annotation-layer content. citeturn964825view1

### What JavaScript actions exist on the page?

- `pdfPage.getJSActions()`
- This returns page-level JS actions. citeturn964825view1

### What operators actually draw the page?

- `pdfPage.getOperatorList()`
- This returns the page’s operator list. Use it when you need the page-description layer rather than just extracted text or structure. citeturn964825view1

### Is this page XFA-based?

- `pdfPage.getXfa()`
- This returns a fake DOM-like tree for XFA content, or `null` if no XFA exists. That matters for forms-heavy PDFs that are not ordinary AcroForm-only documents. citeturn964825view1

### Basic page geometry

- `pdfPage.pageNumber`
- `pdfPage.rotate`
- `pdfPage.view`
- `pdfPage.userUnit`
- `pdfPage.ref`
- These expose page identity and geometry, which are useful for layout analysis and page mapping. citeturn964825view1

## The clean mapping from question to API

### “Is there real text here?”

- `getTextContent()` or `streamTextContent()` citeturn964825view1

### “Is the PDF tagged?”

- `getMarkInfo()` first, then `getStructTree()` page by page. citeturn367400view1turn964825view1

### “What semantic/tag structure exists?”

- `getStructTree()` page by page. citeturn964825view1

### “What links/widgets/annotations exist?”

- `getAnnotations()` citeturn964825view1

### “What forms exist?”

- `getFieldObjects()`
- `getXfa()` for XFA cases. citeturn367400view4turn964825view1

### “What bookmarks/navigation exist?”

- `getOutline()` citeturn367400view2

### “What metadata/title-ish info exists?”

- `getMetadata()` citeturn367400view0

### “What permissions/security flags exist?”

- `getPermissions()` citeturn367400view3

### “What is the page actually doing at the content-stream level?”

- `getOperatorList()` citeturn964825view1

## What PDF.js still does not give you cleanly

### OCR

PDF.js is not the OCR step. Its documented role is parsing/interpreting PDFs in the core layer and exposing a display-layer API to render PDFs and get information out of them. For scanned/image-only PDFs, you need another OCR-capable tool. citeturn964825view0turn964825view1

### A single documented whole-document structure-tree call

In the public docs I checked, `getStructTree()` is on **`PDFPageProxy`**, not `PDFDocumentProxy`. So structure extraction is page-by-page in the documented API. citeturn964825view1turn964825view2

### A stable public low-level object-model API

PDF.js explicitly says the **core layer** is advanced and likely to change, and that the documented API is the display layer. So if you want a supported public surface, stick to the display-layer APIs above. citeturn964825view0

### Human judgment

PDF.js can tell you what is present. It does not tell you whether the reading order is truly good, whether alt text is useful, or whether the file is genuinely accessible in practice. That part still requires judgment and testing. fileciteturn0file1 fileciteturn0file3

If you want, I can turn this into a literal **developer cheat sheet** with example code for each PDF.js call.

Yes, but **not in the open-source JavaScript stack I could verify as a clean, documented, stable public API**.

## Open-source JavaScript answer

Within **open-source JS**, I could verify this:

- **PDF.js** gives you **page-level** structure extraction with `getStructTree()` on `PDFPageProxy`, not a documented one-call **whole-document** structure-tree API on `PDFDocumentProxy`. citeturn506130search4
- PDF.js’s own docs say the **core layer** is where the binary PDF is parsed and interpreted, but that using it directly is **advanced** and the API is **likely to change**. So there may be internal ways to walk more of the document structure, but that is not the supported public surface. citeturn506130search4
- **unpdf** is built on PDF.js and advertises extraction of **text, links, and images** across runtimes; I did not find a separate advertised API there for full-document tag-tree extraction. citeturn519125search8turn506130search4
- **Scribe.js** is about OCR/text extraction and searchable text layers, not whole-document semantic tag-tree extraction. citeturn519125search7

So the tight answer is:

**I did not find another open-source JavaScript library with a clearly documented public API for extracting the entire PDF structure tree/tags for the whole document in one shot.** The closest mainstream open-source option remains **PDF.js**, but its documented structure-tree extraction is page-by-page, and deeper whole-document access appears to live in its advanced internal/core layer rather than a stable public API. citeturn506130search4

## Outside open source

If you ever relax the open-source-only constraint, there **are** JavaScript SDKs that explicitly advertise logical-structure access for tagged PDFs at the document level. For example, Apryse has a **Logical Structure Reader** sample that explores the **StructTree** of a tagged PDF and dumps it to the console. citeturn519125search11turn519125search5

## Practical conclusion

For **open-source JS**:

- **Best documented structure extraction**: **PDF.js**
- **Whole-document structure tree in one clean public API**: **I could not verify one**
- **OCR/text layer help**: **Scribe.js**
- **Another open-source JS library that clearly beats PDF.js on full tag-tree extraction**: **I could not verify one** citeturn506130search4turn519125search7turn519125search8

If you want, I can do one more pass and check the **PDF.js source itself** to see whether there is an internal whole-document structure root you could reach from the core layer, with the caveat that it would be unsupported.

- **Your own traversal over the parsed PDF object graph**

For **PDF.js**, the split is explicit in its own docs:

- The **display layer** is the supported public API for rendering and getting information out of a document. citeturn837400view0turn837400view1turn837400view2
- The **core layer** is where the binary PDF is parsed and interpreted, but PDF.js says using it directly is **advanced usage** and the API is **likely to change**. It even points to a **PDF Object Browser** example for that kind of access. citeturn837400view0
- The repo also exposes a **PDF debugger** that can “browse the internal structure of a PDF document.” citeturn517298view0

So the real answer is:

## If you require a **stable documented interface**

Then yes, you care about the public API, and in that world PDF.js gives you:

- document-level things like metadata, MarkInfo, outline, permissions, and form objects
- page-level `getStructTree()` for structure/tags on each page, not a documented whole-document `getStructTree()` on `PDFDocumentProxy` citeturn837400view1turn517298view3turn517298view2

## If you do **not** require a public API

Then you can go deeper:

- use the **core layer**
- use the **PDF debugger**
- use the **PDF Object Browser** example
- inspect internal parsed objects and build your own whole-document structure extraction

The tradeoff is straightforward:

- **Public API** = safer, documented, more stable
- **Internal/core access** = deeper, but brittle and subject to change citeturn837400view0turn517298view0

So no, it does **not** have to be API-level in the official sense. It only has to be public API if you want something stable and supported.

If you want, I can map the three concrete routes for **whole-document tag extraction in PDF.js**:
**supported public path**, **internal/core path**, and **debugger/source path**.

## The difference in plain English

Think of it like this:

### Public API

This is the part the library **officially gives you to use**.

It is:

- documented
- intended for developers
- more stable across versions
- the part maintainers are signaling “use this”

For PDF.js, examples are methods like:

- `getTextContent()`
- `getStructTree()`
- `getMetadata()`
- `getOutline()`
- `getPermissions()`\
  These live in the documented API pages. ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/api/draft/module-pdfjsLib-PDFPageProxy.html)) ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/api/draft/module-pdfjsLib-PDFDocumentProxy.html))

### Internal / core layer

This is the part the library uses **under the hood** to make the public API work.

It is:

- deeper
- less documented for normal use
- more likely to change
- not something the maintainers are promising to keep stable for you

PDF.js says this directly: the **core layer** is where the binary PDF is parsed and interpreted, and using it directly is **advanced usage** and **likely to change**. ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/getting_started/))

### Debugger / source path

This means you are not relying on the nice official methods at all. You are instead:

- reading the source code
- using the project’s debug tools
- inspecting internal objects yourself
- building your own extraction logic on top of those internals

PDF.js explicitly ships a **PDF debugger** for browsing the internal structure of a PDF document. ([github.com](https://github.com/mozilla/pdf.js/))

---

## The practical difference

### Public API path

You ask the library:

- “Give me the text”
- “Give me the page structure tree”
- “Give me the outline”

And it gives you answers through official methods.

This is the safe path.

### Internal/core path

You are digging into the engine and saying:

- “Show me the parsed PDF objects”
- “Show me the raw structure information before it gets packaged nicely”
- “Let me walk the internal object graph myself”

This is the powerful but fragile path.

### Debugger/source path

You are basically acting like a mechanic opening the machine instead of using the dashboard.

---

## Why this matters for your question

You asked whether another JS tool can pull the **whole document** structure tree.

The reason I kept mentioning “public API” is:

- **PDF.js public API** gives you **page-level** `getStructTree()`
- I did **not** find a documented **whole-document** structure-tree method in that public API
- that does **not** mean the information is impossible to reach
- it means you may have to go through **internals**, **debug tools**, or **source-level traversal** instead of one clean official call ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/api/draft/module-pdfjsLib-PDFPageProxy.html)) ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/getting_started/))

---

## Tiny analogy

### Public API

Like a car dashboard:

- speed
- fuel
- temperature

### Internal/core

Like opening the hood:

- engine parts
- wiring
- raw signals

### Debugger/source path

Like taking apart the engine with the service manual open.

---

## For you, the real distinction is

If you want:

### “Something I can rely on in a normal project”

Use the **public API**.

### “Maximum access, even if I have to do weird unsupported stuff”

Use **internals/source/debug tooling**.

So when I said “I didn’t find a public API,” I did **not** mean “the data is definitely unreachable.”

I meant:
**I didn’t find a clean, officially supported method for it.**

If you want, I’ll now answer the next question directly:

**“Given that I do not care whether it is public API, what are the actual ways to get the whole-document structure/tags in JavaScript?”**

#### ChatGPT:
Yes, it **is** weird.

And your instinct is sound for two reasons:

- In PDF itself, the **tag tree is a document-level thing**, not a page-level concept. The PDF Association glossary describes the **tag tree** as a hierarchy of the **complete real content of a document** that communicates the logical content order to software, and Adobe’s logical structure docs describe the root as the **Structure Tree Root** for the document. citeturn219493search6turn219493search2
- In **PDF.js**, the documented public method is `getStructTree()` on **`PDFPageProxy`**, so the supported extraction surface is page-by-page rather than “give me the whole document structure tree.” citeturn528234search1

So the mismatch is real:

| Thing                                | Reality                                                                                       |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **PDF spec model**                   | Structure is fundamentally **document-level**. citeturn219493search6turn219493search2     |
| **PDF.js public extraction surface** | Structure-tree access is exposed **per page** in the documented API. citeturn528234search1 |

Why this probably happened:

- **PDF.js is primarily a viewer/parser project**, and its stated goal is a “general-purpose, web standards-based platform for parsing and rendering PDFs.” That tends to push the public API toward page-oriented operations, because viewers render and inspect pages incrementally. citeturn528234search1
- PDF.js also explicitly separates the supported **display layer** from the deeper **core layer**, and says direct use of the core layer is advanced and likely to change. So they seem to have chosen to expose a cleaner, narrower public surface rather than a full document-graph extraction API. citeturn528234search1

That second bullet is partly an inference from how the project describes itself and how the API is shaped, but I think it is the most plausible reading.

So the clean answer is:

- **Yes, it is weird**
- **No, you are not misunderstanding the PDF model**
- **The likely reason is library design, not that whole-document structure doesn’t exist**

And right now, within the open-source JS space we checked, I still do **not** have a verified example of a clean, documented library that exposes the **entire document tag tree** in one supported call better than PDF.js does page-by-page. citeturn528234search1

The one thing PDF.js does have that points deeper is its **PDF debugger**, which the repo describes as letting you browse the internal structure of a PDF document. That suggests the information is there in the engine even though the public API stays page-oriented. citeturn528234search1

If you want, the next useful step is: I can check **whether PDF.js’s internal debugger/core layer actually exposes the document-level Structure Tree Root in a way you could script against**, even if it is unsupported.

Yes — I checked for that specific thing.

**Within open-source JavaScript, I could not verify a documented package/library that cleanly exposes the entire document tag tree / structure tree in one supported call.** The closest mainstream option is still **PDF.js**, but its documented `getStructTree()` lives on **`PDFPageProxy`** — page by page — while document-level APIs cover things like MarkInfo, metadata, outline, permissions, and form field objects instead. PDF.js also says its deeper **core layer** is advanced and likely to change, which is why I kept separating “supported API” from internals. ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/api/draft/module-pdfjsLib-PDFPageProxy.html)) ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/api/draft/module-pdfjsLib-PDFDocumentProxy.html)) ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/getting_started/))

The extraction packages I found in the open-source JS ecosystem did **not** change that answer. **pdf.js-extract** documents text extraction with coordinates and explicitly says “extracts text from PDF files.” **pdf-extraction** documents text, page count, info, metadata, and custom page rendering. **pdfdataextract** documents text, outline, metadata, and permissions. None of those package docs I found advertise whole-document tag-tree / StructTree extraction. ([github.com](https://github.com/ffalt/pdf.js-extract)) ([npmjs.com](https://www.npmjs.com/package/pdf-extraction)) ([npmjs.com](https://www.npmjs.com/package/pdfdataextract))

I also checked the open-source **PDFium** wrappers I found for JavaScript. The current `@hyzyla/pdfium` project describes itself as a TypeScript/JavaScript wrapper for PDFium, emphasizes rendering/performance, and its README usage example is about loading a document and rendering pages. In the docs/pages I checked, I did **not** find documented whole-document structure-tree/tag extraction there either. ([github.com](https://github.com/hyzyla/pdfium))

So the direct answer to your actual question is:

- **Open-source JS package that I could verify does whole-document tag/tree extraction cleanly:** **I did not find one**
- **Best documented open-source JS option overall for tag-related extraction:** **PDF.js**, but **page by page**, not one whole-document call ([mozilla.github.io](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/api/draft/module-pdfjsLib-PDFPageProxy.html))

One important contrast: **outside** the open-source-only constraint, there **is** a JavaScript SDK that does the whole-document structure tree directly. Apryse’s JavaScript sample calls `doc.getStructTree()` and then walks the whole tagged document structure recursively. ([docs.apryse.com](https://docs.apryse.com/web/samples/logicalstructuretest))

So the cleaned-up answer is:

- **Open-source JS:** I could not verify one
- **JavaScript in general:** yes, but the one I could verify is commercial, not open source ([docs.apryse.com](https://docs.apryse.com/web/samples/logicalstructuretest))

If you want, I’ll do the same exact check for **Python**, where the odds are better.

Make sure to include fileciteturn6file0 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.
## Scope

This is a **creation-only generator requirements spec** for a controlled PDF pipeline.

It is designed to make the previously identified Matterhorn human-judgment conditions **deterministic by construction** by moving them upstream into:

* explicit source-model fields
* explicit asset requirements
* explicit serialization rules
* explicit validation gates

This is **assistant synthesis** built on the Matterhorn Protocol’s human-judgment conditions and the PDF accessibility guidance you uploaded about accessible source structure, tags, reading order, figures, metadata, and forms. It is meant for a generator that owns the semantic model and the PDF writing step. ([pdfa.org](https://pdfa.org/download-area/publications/Matterhorn-Protocol-1-1.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com))   

## What this spec is for

This spec is intended to make these kinds of conditions deterministic:

* structure and role correctness
* tag presence
* reading order
* heading/list/table/formula/note/reference tagging
* figure and caption handling
* artifact handling
* annotation order and function
* form-related structural requirements
* script and media constraints
* OCR policy constraints
* font embedding constraints

It does **not** fully solve content-quality questions like whether a title “clearly identifies” the document or whether a description is actually good enough for humans. Those remain outside pure serialization control. ([pdfa.org](https://pdfa.org/download-area/publications/Matterhorn-Protocol-1-1.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

---

# Generator requirements spec

## 1. System assumptions

Your generator must enforce all of these assumptions:

* The generator does **not** infer semantics from appearance.
* Every output element comes from an explicit source-model node type.
* Every node type has a defined PDF tag mapping.
* Every node appears in a single canonical reading order sequence before serialization.
* Decorative and structural content are modeled separately.
* The generator either owns all embedded assets or rejects assets that do not pass required checks.
* The generator either forbids unsupported features or requires explicit equivalents before output. ([pdfa.org](https://pdfa.org/download-area/publications/Matterhorn-Protocol-1-1.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com))  

## 2. Required source-model fields

Every document must provide these top-level fields:

* `document_title`
* `document_language`
* `document_identifier`
* `document_version`
* `reading_order` as an explicit ordered tree or ordered node list
* `bookmarks` or rules for deriving them from headings
* `role_map` if any custom semantic roles are allowed
* `uses_javascript` as explicit true or false
* `uses_multimedia` as explicit true or false
* `uses_ocr_text` as explicit true or false
* `embedded_fonts` manifest
* `annotations` list
* `article_threads` list if article threads are used
* `form_mode` with allowed values like `none`, `interactive`, `non_interactive_print_fields`

Every content node must provide:

* `node_id`
* `node_type`
* `parent_id`
* `children`
* `reading_order_index`
* `is_artifact`
* `language` if it differs from document language
* `visual_bbox` if layout-aware output is needed
* `page_assignment`
* `tag_required` true or false
* `content_source`

Node types must be explicit and closed, not freeform. At minimum:

* `heading`
* `paragraph`
* `list`
* `list_item`
* `list_label`
* `list_body`
* `table`
* `table_head`
* `table_body`
* `table_row`
* `table_header_cell`
* `table_data_cell`
* `figure`
* `caption`
* `formula`
* `note`
* `reference`
* `link`
* `annotation_anchor`
* `form_field`
* `artifact_header`
* `artifact_footer`
* `artifact_other`

This follows the same underlying logic as the guidance in your uploaded sources: accessible PDFs depend on explicit structure, tags, reading order, figures, metadata, and form behavior rather than appearance alone.   

---

## 3. Serialization rules

## 3.1 Structure and artifact rules

The serializer must:

* create a structure tree for all non-artifact content
* serialize only artifact nodes as artifacts
* prevent any semantic node from being artifacted
* prevent any artifact node from entering the semantic structure tree
* forbid untyped content nodes
* forbid orphaned semantic nodes
* forbid nodes with ambiguous parentage

This directly addresses artifact-versus-real-content failures and general structure-type failures. ([pdfa.org](https://pdfa.org/download-area/publications/Matterhorn-Protocol-1-1.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

### Covers

* 01-001
* 01-002
* 01-006
* 02-002

## 3.2 Reading order rules

The serializer must:

* consume one explicit canonical reading order
* serialize tags in that exact order
* serialize annotation order from that same order
* serialize article threads only if they match the canonical order
* reject content trees whose semantic order conflicts with their declared order
* reject ambiguous ordering at authoring time instead of guessing

### Covers

* 09-001
* 22-001
* 28-001

## 3.3 Tag mapping rules

Every semantic node type must map to one allowed PDF structure type or a documented role-mapped equivalent.

Examples:

* `heading(level=1)` → `H1`
* `heading(level=7)` → `H7`
* `paragraph` → `P`
* `list` → `L`
* `list_item` → `LI`
* `caption` → `Caption`
* `formula` → `Formula`
* `note` → `Note`
* `reference` → `Reference`
* `figure` → `Figure`

The generator must reject:

* heading levels without a valid mapping
* non-standard roles without an explicit valid role map
* illegal nesting patterns
* semantic nodes whose mapped role conflicts with the declared node type

### Covers

* 09-002
* 09-003
* 14-001
* 14-004
* 14-005
* 16-003
* 17-001
* 19-001
* 19-002

## 3.4 Headers, footers, and pagination artifacts

If repeated page headers or footers are present, the source model must classify them explicitly as pagination artifacts.

The serializer must:

* artifact them
* subtype them as `Header` or `Footer` where required
* prevent them from entering the semantic reading order

### Covers

* 18-001
* 18-002

## 3.5 Figure, caption, and grouped graphic rules

For every graphic-bearing node, the source model must declare one of:

* `decorative`
* `informative_figure`
* `text_as_figure`
* `group_member_figure`
* `link_with_meaningful_background`

The serializer must then enforce:

* informative graphics are tagged as `Figure`
* captions are tagged as `Caption`
* grouped graphics are not exposed as semantically separate when their meaning only exists as a group
* text-as-figure cases use the intended text mechanism rather than generic figure handling
* decorative graphics are artifacts, not figures

### Covers

* 13-001
* 13-003
* 13-005
* 13-006
* 13-008

## 3.6 Table rules

The source model must represent tables structurally, not visually.

Required table fields:

* `table_id`
* `rows`
* `columns`
* `header_scope`
* `header_associations`
* `is_data_table`
* `is_layout_table` forbidden for semantic `table`

The serializer must:

* reject any semantic table node that is not actually row/column structured
* tag row headers and column headers as headers
* require unambiguous header associations for every data cell
* reject cells without defined header relationships where relationships are required

### Covers

* 15-001
* 15-002
* 15-004
* 15-005

## 3.7 List rules

The source model must distinguish:

* ordered list
* unordered list
* custom ordered scheme

Required fields:

* `list_kind`
* `list_numbering_style` when ordered
* list item sequence

The serializer must:

* require `ListNumbering` for ordered lists
* reject invalid numbering values
* prevent list-looking content from being serialized as plain paragraphs when modeled as lists

### Covers

* 16-001
* 16-002
* 16-003

## 3.8 Heading rules

The source model must carry:

* heading text
* heading level
* bookmark inclusion flag

The serializer must:

* emit heading tags for all headings
* map heading levels deterministically
* reject unsupported heading-level policies
* derive bookmarks from heading nodes when configured

### Covers

* 14-001
* 14-004
* 14-005

## 3.9 Form and annotation rules

If forms are present, the source model must declare:

* `interactive` or `non_interactive`
* field list
* field order
* field names
* field labels
* annotation semantics
* any visual-only annotation function

The serializer must:

* emit non-interactive print fields only when declared and with required attributes
* place annotations in semantic reading order
* tag annotation-backed content according to semantic function, not visual effect
* reject annotation-only visual hacks without semantic mapping

### Covers

* 24-001
* 28-001
* 28-003

## 3.10 Scripting and timing rules

The generator must adopt a strict scripting policy.

Allowed policies:

* `no_javascript`
* `restricted_javascript`

If JavaScript is allowed, the generator must forbid:

* flicker-producing actions
* beep-only notification
* required keystroke timing behavior

### Covers

* 03-001
* 03-003
* 05-003
* 29-001

---

## 4. Asset and content gates

These are not just serialization rules. They are **input gates**.

## 4.1 Multimedia gate

Every audio or media asset must include required equivalents before build.

Required fields:

* `transcript`
* `alternate_form_available`
* `flicker_checked`

Build must fail if:

* audio exists without required alternate form
* media exists without required alternate form
* flicker policy is violated

### Covers

* 03-002
* 05-001
* 05-002

## 4.2 OCR gate

Best policy for a controlled creation pipeline:

* **forbid OCR as a content source unless explicitly approved**

If OCR is allowed, require:

* `ocr_source`
* `ocr_confidence`
* `ocr_review_status`
* `ocr_tagging_status`

Build must fail if:

* OCR text is below approved confidence threshold
* OCR text is unreviewed when review is required
* OCR text is not tagged

### Covers

* 08-001
* 08-002

## 4.3 Link-background gate

If a link uses a meaningful background graphic, require explicit fields for both:

* link purpose
* background graphic purpose

Build must fail if either is missing.

### Covers

* 13-002

## 4.4 Font gate

Every embedded font must have a pre-build license flag:

* `embeddable_for_unlimited_universal_rendering` true or false

Build must fail if false.

### Covers

* 31-010

## 4.5 Image map gate

If `IsMap=true` or equivalent image-map-like behavior is used, the source model must include:

* equivalent non-pointer-dependent interaction path

Build must fail if absent.

### Covers

* 28-013

---

## 5. Metadata and language rules

The source model must provide:

* document title
* document language
* per-node language overrides where needed

The serializer must:

* write title metadata
* write language metadata
* write language changes where the source model marks them
* reject empty required metadata fields

This makes presence deterministic. It does **not** prove the title is clear enough or the language choice is substantively appropriate in every nuanced case.

### Covers deterministically

* 11-007 only to the extent of explicit language assignment and serialization

### Does not fully solve content-quality judgment

* 06-004
* part of 11-007

---

## 6. Representation-choice policy

Your generator must carry an explicit representation policy for complex content.

For each complex graphic or visually dependent construct, the source model must declare one of:

* keep as figure with text alternative
* render as accessible text/table/formula instead
* provide alternate accessible representation alongside figure
* reject unsupported construct

This can make representation choice **policy-deterministic** inside your system, but it does not prove that your policy is always the best accessible choice in the abstract.

### Helps control

* 13-007

### Still partly a judgment issue

* whether the chosen representation is truly the most accessible one for all users

---

## 7. Build-time rejection rules

The generator must **fail the build** when any of these occur:

* semantic node with no allowed PDF tag mapping
* artifact node entering semantic structure
* semantic node marked as artifact
* missing reading-order index
* ambiguous table header associations
* ordered list with missing or invalid numbering metadata
* figure missing required representation classification
* caption node not attached to parent figure/content object as required
* media without required alternate form
* OCR content without approved gate status
* JavaScript violating timing or notification policy
* unsupported or unlicensed font embedding
* missing equivalent path for image-map behavior
* unsupported heading level without explicit mapping
* untagged OCR content
* non-interactive form output without required print-field declaration

This is the core mechanism that turns “human check later” into “generator makes it impossible.”

---

## 8. Post-build validation gates

Even with deterministic construction, the pipeline should still run:

* structural validation
* PDF/UA machine-checkable validation
* internal consistency checks between source model and emitted PDF

Matterhorn and veraPDF matter here because they define the line between what software can verify from the finished file and what still remains human-review territory. ([pdfa.org](https://pdfa.org/download-area/publications/Matterhorn-Protocol-1-1.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ([docs.verapdf.org](https://docs.verapdf.org/validation/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

---

# Coverage map

## Fully deterministic by construction with this spec

These are the ones this spec is designed to make deterministic:

* 01-001
* 01-002
* 01-006
* 02-002
* 03-001
* 03-003
* 04-001
* 05-003
* 08-002
* 09-001
* 09-002
* 09-003
* 12-001
* 13-001
* 13-003
* 13-006
* 14-001
* 14-004
* 14-005
* 15-001
* 15-002
* 15-004
* 15-005
* 16-001
* 16-002
* 16-003
* 17-001
* 18-001
* 18-002
* 19-001
* 19-002
* 22-001
* 24-001
* 28-001
* 28-003
* 28-013
* 29-001
* 31-010

## Deterministic if the generator also owns assets or richer content inputs

* 03-002
* 05-001
* 05-002
* 08-001
* 13-002
* 13-005
* 13-008
* 11-007

## Still not fully provable by serialization alone

* 06-004
* 13-007
* the quality side of 13-002
* the quality side of 05-001 and 05-002
* the nuanced content-quality side of 11-007

---

## The shortest usable version

To make those Matterhorn conditions deterministic in a controlled generator, your pipeline needs:

* an explicit semantic source model
* explicit artifact classification
* explicit reading order
* explicit tag mapping rules
* explicit table/list/figure/form structures
* explicit asset gates for media, OCR, fonts, and image maps
* strict rejection rules instead of inference
* machine validation after build

That is the actual bridge from “human judgment in generic validation” to “deterministic by construction in a controlled creator.”

I can turn this into a tighter **JSON-schema-style field contract** next.

Below is a **paraphrased** list of the **87 Matterhorn Protocol 1.1 failure conditions whose `How` value is `M`** — meaning the protocol treats them as determinable by software alone. I’m **not** including the two “no specific tests” rows, **23-001** and **27-001**, because those are not part of the 87 machine-determinable failures. ([PDF Association][1])

## Checkpoint 01: Real content tagged

* **01-003** — Artifact-marked content appears inside tagged content.
* **01-004** — Tagged content appears inside artifact-marked content.
* **01-005** — Content is neither marked as artifact nor tagged as real content.
* **01-007** — The `Suspects` entry is set to `true`. 

## Checkpoint 02: Role mapping

* **02-001** — A non-standard tag’s role mapping does not terminate in a standard type.
* **02-003** — A circular role mapping exists.
* **02-004** — One or more standard structure types are remapped. 

## Checkpoint 06: Metadata

* **06-001** — The document has no XMP metadata stream.
* **06-002** — The Catalog’s XMP metadata does not include the PDF/UA identifier.
* **06-003** — The XMP metadata does not include `dc:title`. 

## Checkpoint 07: Dictionary

* **07-001** — `ViewerPreferences` is missing `DisplayDocTitle`.
* **07-002** — `ViewerPreferences.DisplayDocTitle` is present but set to `false`. 

## Checkpoint 09: Appropriate tags

* **09-004** — A table-related structure element is used in a way that violates ISO 32000-1 Table 337 syntax.
* **09-005** — A list-related structure element is used in a way that violates ISO 32000-1 Table 336 syntax.
* **09-006** — A TOC-related structure element is used in a way that violates ISO 32000-1 Table 333 syntax.
* **09-007** — A Ruby-related structure element is used in a way that violates ISO 32000-1 Table 338 syntax.
* **09-008** — A Warichu-related structure element is used in a way that violates ISO 32000-1 Table 338 syntax. 

## Checkpoint 10: Character mappings

* **10-001** — A character code cannot be mapped to Unicode. 

## Checkpoint 11: Declared natural language

* **11-001** — The natural language for page text cannot be determined.
* **11-002** — The natural language for `Alt`, `ActualText`, and `E` attributes cannot be determined.
* **11-003** — The natural language of outline entries cannot be determined.
* **11-004** — The natural language in annotation `Contents` cannot be determined.
* **11-005** — The natural language in a form field’s `TU` entry cannot be determined.
* **11-006** — The natural language for document metadata cannot be determined. 

## Checkpoint 13: Graphics

* **13-004** — A `<Figure>` tag is missing required alternative text or replacement text. 

## Checkpoint 14: Headings

* **14-002** — The document uses numbered headings, but the first heading tag is not `<H1>`.
* **14-003** — Heading levels skip downward in sequence, such as jumping from `<H1>` directly to `<H3>`.
* **14-006** — A node contains more than one `<H>` tag.
* **14-007** — The document mixes `<H>` tags with `<H#>` tags. ([PDF Association][1])

## Checkpoint 15: Tables

* **15-003** — In a table that is not organized using `Headers`/`IDs`, a `<TH>` cell is missing a `Scope` attribute. 

## Checkpoint 17: Mathematical expressions

* **17-002** — A `<Formula>` tag is missing an `Alt` attribute.
* **17-003** — Unicode mapping requirements are not met for the formula content. 

## Checkpoint 19: Notes and references

* **19-003** — A `<Note>` tag is missing its `ID`.
* **19-004** — A `<Note>` tag’s `ID` is not unique. 

## Checkpoint 20: Optional content

* **20-001** — An optional-content configuration in `Configs` is missing `Name` or has an empty `Name`.
* **20-002** — The default optional-content configuration in `D` is missing `Name` or has an empty `Name`.
* **20-003** — An `AS` entry appears in an optional-content configuration dictionary. 

## Checkpoint 21: Embedded files

* **21-001** — An embedded file specification dictionary is missing `F` and `UF` entries. 

## Checkpoint 25: XFA

* **25-001** — The file contains the `dynamicRender` element with value `required`. ([PDF Association][1])

## Checkpoint 26: Security

* **26-001** — The file is encrypted but has no `P` entry in its encryption dictionary.
* **26-002** — The file is encrypted, has a `P` entry, but the 10th bit of `P` is `false`. ([PDF Association][1])

## Checkpoint 28: Annotations

* **28-002** — An annotation other than Widget, Link, or PrinterMark is not a direct child of an `<Annot>` structure element.
* **28-004** — A non-Widget annotation lacks a `Contents` entry and also lacks an alternative description.
* **28-005** — A form field lacks a `TU` entry and also lacks an alternative description.
* **28-006** — An annotation with a subtype not defined in ISO 32000 fails the requirements of 7.18.1.
* **28-007** — A `TrapNet` annotation exists.
* **28-008** — A page containing an annotation has no `Tabs` entry.
* **28-009** — A page containing an annotation has a `Tabs` entry whose value is not `S`.
* **28-010** — A widget annotation is not nested inside a `<Form>` tag.
* **28-011** — A link annotation is not nested inside a `<Link>` tag.
* **28-012** — A link annotation lacks an alternate description in its `Contents` entry.
* **28-014** — The media clip data dictionary is missing `CT`.
* **28-015** — The media clip data dictionary is missing `Alt`.
* **28-016** — A file attachment annotation does not conform to checkpoint 7.11 requirements.
* **28-017** — A PrinterMark annotation is included in the logical structure.
* **28-018** — The appearance stream of a PrinterMark annotation is not marked as artifact. ([PDF Association][1])

## Checkpoint 30: XObjects

* **30-001** — A reference XObject is present.
* **30-002** — A Form XObject contains MCIDs and is referenced more than once. ([PDF Association][1])

## Checkpoint 31: Fonts

* **31-001** — In a Type 0 font with non-Identity encoding, the `Registry` values in the two `CIDSystemInfo` dictionaries do not match.
* **31-002** — In a Type 0 font with non-Identity encoding, the `Ordering` values in the two `CIDSystemInfo` dictionaries do not match.
* **31-003** — In a Type 0 font with non-Identity encoding, the CID font’s `Supplement` value is lower than the CMap’s `Supplement` value.
* **31-004** — A Type 2 CID font has neither a `CIDToGIDMap` stream nor `Identity` as the `CIDToGIDMap` value.
* **31-005** — A Type 2 CID font is missing `CIDToGIDMap`.
* **31-006** — A CMap is neither one of the standard listed CMaps nor embedded.
* **31-007** — The `WMode` in a CMap dictionary does not match the `WMode` in the CMap stream.
* **31-008** — A CMap references another CMap that is not on the standard list.
* **31-009** — A font program used for rendered text is not embedded.
* **31-011** — An embedded font program is missing one or more glyphs that the rendered text uses.
* **31-012** — An embedded Type 1 font’s `CharSet` omits at least one glyph that is present in the font program.
* **31-013** — An embedded Type 1 font’s `CharSet` names at least one glyph that is not present in the font program.
* **31-014** — An embedded CID font’s `CIDSet` omits at least one glyph that is present in the font program.
* **31-015** — An embedded CID font’s `CIDSet` names at least one glyph that is not present in the font program.
* **31-016** — Glyph width information in the font dictionary and the embedded font program differs by more than 1/1000 unit.
* **31-017** — A non-symbolic TrueType font is used for rendering, but the embedded font has no non-symbolic cmap.
* **31-018** — A non-symbolic TrueType font is used for rendering, but at least one rendered glyph cannot be looked up through any non-symbolic cmap.
* **31-019** — A non-symbolic TrueType font dictionary is missing `Encoding`.
* **31-020** — A non-symbolic TrueType font’s `Encoding` dictionary is missing `BaseEncoding`.
* **31-021** — For a non-symbolic TrueType font, `Encoding` or `BaseEncoding` is neither `MacRomanEncoding` nor `WinAnsiEncoding`.
* **31-022** — The `Differences` array in a non-symbolic TrueType font names one or more glyphs that are not in the Adobe Glyph List.
* **31-023** — A `Differences` array is present in a non-symbolic TrueType font, but the embedded font program lacks a `(3,1)` Microsoft Unicode cmap.
* **31-024** — A symbolic TrueType font dictionary contains an `Encoding` entry.
* **31-025** — An embedded symbolic TrueType font contains no cmap.
* **31-026** — An embedded symbolic TrueType font contains more than one cmap, but none is a `(3,0)` Microsoft Symbol cmap.
* **31-027** — A font dictionary lacks `ToUnicode`, and none of the protocol’s allowed exception cases applies.
* **31-028** — One or more Unicode values in the `ToUnicode` CMap are zero.
* **31-029** — One or more Unicode values in the `ToUnicode` CMap are `U+FEFF` or `U+FFFE`.
* **31-030** — One or more displayed text characters reference the `.notdef` glyph. ([PDF Association][1])

That is the full machine-determinable set: **87 rows marked `M`** in Matterhorn Protocol 1.1. ([PDF Association][1])

If you want the next pass, the useful one is: **which of these 87 are trivially enforceable in a controlled generator, which need low-level PDF checks, and which are mainly font/encoding edge cases**.

[1]: https://pdfa.org/download-area/publications/Matterhorn-Protocol-1-1.pdf "Matterhorn Protocol 1.1 - PDF/UA Conformance Testing Model"

#### ChatGPT:
This helps. It changes the shape of the problem.

Your pasted document is **not** an “analyze arbitrary existing PDFs” spec. It is a **creation-only generator spec** for a controlled pipeline, where accessibility is made deterministic by construction through an explicit source model, explicit serialization rules, explicit asset gates, and build-time rejection rules. fileciteturn7file0

## What that means

In this model, the generator:

- does **not** infer semantics from appearance
- requires every node to have an explicit type
- requires one canonical reading order
- separates semantic content from artifacts
- maps every semantic node to an allowed PDF tag
- fails the build instead of guessing when structure is ambiguous fileciteturn7file3 fileciteturn7file2

That is a very different problem from:

- “Can JavaScript inspect the whole tag tree of an arbitrary PDF?”
- “Can JavaScript reconstruct missing semantics from an existing file?”

## The core idea in your spec

The spec is basically saying:

> Don’t treat accessibility as something you discover after the PDF exists.\
> Treat it as something you **own upstream** and then serialize deterministically.

That is why it requires:

- explicit `reading_order`
- explicit `document_title`
- explicit `document_language`
- explicit `role_map`
- explicit node types like `heading`, `paragraph`, `list`, `table`, `figure`, `caption`, `formula`, `note`, `reference`, `link`, `form_field`
- explicit artifact types like `artifact_header`, `artifact_footer`, `artifact_other` fileciteturn7file3

And then it forces the serializer to:

- create a structure tree for all non-artifact content
- serialize tags in canonical order
- reject illegal or ambiguous mappings
- reject broken table/list/figure/form structures
- reject unsupported OCR/media/font/image-map cases at build time fileciteturn7file3 fileciteturn7file2

## Why this is stronger than normal PDF checking

Your uploaded PDF accessibility sources are all basically describing a world where existing PDFs often fail because they are missing tags, have bad reading order, or depend too much on visual appearance. They also point out that passing basic checks still does not guarantee full accessibility. fileciteturn7file16 fileciteturn7file6 fileciteturn7file8

Your generator spec is trying to remove as much of that uncertainty as possible by saying:

- semantic structure must exist *before* PDF writing
- reading order must exist *before* PDF writing
- artifact classification must exist *before* PDF writing
- unsupported or ambiguous content must fail *before* PDF writing fileciteturn7file4

That is a real improvement in problem framing.

## The important limit your spec already admits

It does **not** claim to solve every accessibility question.

It explicitly says some things remain outside pure serialization control, especially content-quality judgments like:

- whether a title clearly identifies the document
- whether a description is actually good enough for humans
- whether a chosen representation is truly the best accessible representation in context fileciteturn7file0 fileciteturn7file2

That is exactly the honest boundary.

## The clean takeaway

Your pasted spec is saying:

- for a **controlled generator**, a large chunk of PDF accessibility can be made **deterministic by construction**
- that requires a **semantic source model**, not visual inference
- it also requires **strict rejection rules**
- and it still needs **post-build validation**
- but it reduces the “human judgment later” surface substantially fileciteturn7file4

So yes: this is a much better frame than treating accessibility as “run checker after PDF exists and hope for the best.”

The remaining hard question is not “what is accessibility?” anymore. It is:

**Which actual JS creation stack can faithfully serialize this source model into a tagged PDF without dropping the guarantees your spec depends on?**

            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "MENTAL HEALTH TREATMENT",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "body",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;69;p9"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;69;p9",
            "ordered_position": 2,
            "interpreted_text_role": "body_content",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Approximately 19% of U.S. adults, and 13% of adolescents (ages 8-15) experience mental illness in a given year.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "The percentage of adults who received mental health treatment in 2004–2008 is shown below. Adults seeking treatment increased slightly from 2004 to 2008.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "About one-third to one-half of U.S. adolescents with mental disorders receive treatment, with behavior-related disorders more likely to be treated.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {

    {
      "slide_number": 5,
      "observed_source": {

      "normalized_workflow": {
        "chosen_title": "TREATMENT IN THE PAST",
        "title_source": "TITLE_PLACEHOLDER",
        "manual_review_required": true
      },
      "projected_target": {
        "target_package_part": "word/document.xml",
        "projected_slide_heading_text": "Slide 5. TREATMENT IN THE PAST",
        "projected_slide_heading_style": "Heading1",
        "projected_slide_separator_mode": "PAGE_BREAK",
        "projection_status": "planned"
      },
      "warning_entries": [],
      "text_block_entries": [
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "title",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;78;p10"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;78;p10",
            "ordered_position": 1,
            "interpreted_text_role": "title_candidate",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "TREATMENT IN THE PAST",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "body",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;79;p10"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;79;p10",
            "ordered_position": 2,
            "interpreted_text_role": "body_content",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Throughout most of history, mental illness was believed to be caused by supernatural forces such as witchcraft or demonic possession. People with mental illnesses at this time were often subjected to cruelty and poor treatment.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Treatments aimed at supernatural forces:",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "standalone_text_box",
            "placeholder_type": null,
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;82;p10"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;82;p10",
            "ordered_position": 3,
            "interpreted_text_role": "unknown",
            "role_confidence": "low",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [
            {
              "warning_code": "STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN",
              "warning_message": "Standalone text box role is uncertain and should be reviewed before merging into surrounding content.",
              "warning_scope": "text_block",
              "manual_review_required": true
            }
          ],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Exorcism – involving incantations and prayers said over the individual’s body by a priest/religious figure.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Trephining – a hole was made in the skull to release spirits from the body. This often lead to death.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Execution or imprisonment - many mentally ill people were burnt at the stake after being accused of witchcraft.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "standalone_text_box",
            "placeholder_type": null,
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;83;p10"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;83;p10",
            "ordered_position": 4,
            "interpreted_text_role": "unknown",
            "role_confidence": "low",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [
            {
              "warning_code": "STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN",
              "warning_message": "Standalone text box role is uncertain and should be reviewed before merging into surrounding content.",
              "warning_scope": "text_block",
              "manual_review_required": true
            }
          ],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "(Credit: Pinterest – Abnormal psychology)",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        }
      ],

                "text": "18TH CENTURY",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "body",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;90;p11"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;90;p11",
            "ordered_position": 2,
            "interpreted_text_role": "body_content",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "This painting by Francisco Goya, called The Madhouse, depicts a mental asylum and its inhabitants in the early 1800s. It portrays those with psychological disorders as victims.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "standalone_text_box",
            "placeholder_type": null,
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;92;p11"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;92;p11",
            "ordered_position": 3,
            "interpreted_text_role": "unknown",
            "role_confidence": "low",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [
            {
              "warning_code": "STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN",
              "warning_message": "Standalone text box role is uncertain and should be reviewed before merging into surrounding content.",
              "warning_scope": "text_block",
              "manual_review_required": true
            }
          ],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Figure 16.4",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "standalone_text_box",
            "placeholder_type": null,
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;93;p11"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;93;p11",
            "ordered_position": 4,
            "interpreted_text_role": "unknown",
            "role_confidence": "low",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [
            {
              "warning_code": "STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN",
              "warning_message": "Standalone text box role is uncertain and should be reviewed before merging into surrounding content.",
              "warning_scope": "text_block",
              "manual_review_required": true
            }
          ],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "By the 18th century, people exhibiting unusual behavior began to be institutionalized",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Asylums – the first institutions created for the specific purpose of housing people with psychological disorders.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Focus was ostracizing them from society rather than treatment.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Individuals often kept in windowless dungeons, chained to beds, little to no contact with caregivers.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "visual_entries": [
        {
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "label": "Visual 1",
            "visual_type": "picture",
            "description": "OSC-Stacked-TM-RGB-1.png",
            "description_source": "descr",

          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "This painting by Tony Robert-Fleury depicts Dr. Philippe Pinel ordering the removal of chains from patients at the Salpêtrière asylum in Paris.",
                "level": 0,

          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Philippe Pinel (Late 1700s)",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "French physician.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Argued for more humane treatment of the mentally ill.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Suggested that they be unchained and talked to.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Implemented in Paris, 1975.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Patients benefited and many were able to be released from hospital.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]

            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "body",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;110;p13"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;110;p13",
            "ordered_position": 2,
            "interpreted_text_role": "body_content",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Dorothea Dix",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "A social reformer who became an advocate for the indigent insane.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Investigated the state of care for the mentally ill and poor.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Discovered an underfunded and unregulated system that perpetuated abuse of the mentally ill.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Instrumental in creating the first American mental asylum - by relentlessly lobbying state legislatures and Congress to set up and fund such institutions.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "standalone_text_box",
            "placeholder_type": null,
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;112;p13"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;112;p13",
            "ordered_position": 3,
            "interpreted_text_role": "unknown",
            "role_confidence": "low",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [

              "warning_code": "STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN",

              "warning_scope": "text_block",
              "manual_review_required": true

          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Figure 16.6",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }

      },
      "projected_target": {

      "warning_entries": [],
      "text_block_entries": [
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "title",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;117;p14"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;117;p14",
            "ordered_position": 1,
            "interpreted_text_role": "title_candidate",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "19TH CENTURY",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "body",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;118;p14"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;118;p14",
            "ordered_position": 2,
            "interpreted_text_role": "body_content",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "American Asylums",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Usually filthy.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Offered little treatment.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Individuals were often institutionalized for decades.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Treatment:",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Submersion into cold baths for long periods of time.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Electroshock treatment (now called electroconvulsive therapy) – involves a brief application of electric stimulus to produce a generalized seizure.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Conditions such us these were common until well into the 20th century.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "visual_entries": [
        {
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "label": "Visual 1",
            "visual_type": "picture",
            "description": "OSC-Stacked-TM-RGB-1.png",
            "description_source": "descr",
            "manual_review_required": true
          },
          "warning_entries": []
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "slide_number": 10,
      "observed_source": {
        "package_part": "ppt/slides/slide10.xml",

        "chosen_title": "20TH CENTURY",
        "title_source": "TITLE_PLACEHOLDER",
        "manual_review_required": false
      },
      "projected_target": {
        "target_package_part": "word/document.xml",
        "projected_slide_heading_text": "Slide 10. 20TH CENTURY",
        "projected_slide_heading_style": "Heading1",

      },
      "warning_entries": [],
      "text_block_entries": [
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "title",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;124;p15"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;124;p15",
            "ordered_position": 1,
            "interpreted_text_role": "title_candidate",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "20TH CENTURY",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "body",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;125;p15"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;125;p15",
            "ordered_position": 2,
            "interpreted_text_role": "body_content",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "1954 - antipsychotic medications were introduced.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Proved successful in treating symptoms of psychosis.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Psychosis was a common diagnosis, evidenced by symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions, indicating a loss of contact with reality.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "1975 - Mental Retardation Facilities & Community Mental Health Centers Construction Act",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Provided federal support and funding for community mental health centers.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Started the process of deinstitutionalization.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Deinstitutionalization – the closing of large asylums, by providing for people to stay in their communities and be treated locally",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Patients were released but the new system was not set up effectively.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Centers were underfunded, staff untrained to handle severe mental illnesses.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Lead to an increase in homelessness.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "bullet",
                "list_marker_hint": "•"
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "visual_entries": [
        {
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "label": "Visual 1",

            "description": "OSC-Stacked-TM-RGB-1.png",

          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "MENTAL HEALTH TREATMENT TODAY",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "observed_source": {
            "source_text_container_type": "placeholder_text",
            "placeholder_type": "body",
            "shape_name": "Google Shape;133;p16"
          },
          "normalized_workflow": {
            "source_name": "Google Shape;133;p16",
            "ordered_position": 2,
            "interpreted_text_role": "body_content",
            "role_confidence": "high",
            "auto_merge_allowed": false
          },
          "warning_entries": [],
          "paragraph_entries": [
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Mental illness among the homeless population is still common today.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "plain",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": []
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Of the homeless individuals in U.S. shelters, about one-quarter have a severe mental illness (HUD, 2011).",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "number",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": [
                {
                  "warning_code": "NUMBERED_SEQUENCE_REVIEW",
                  "warning_message": "Numbering intent was observed, but ordered sequence should be reviewed in the DOCX.",
                  "warning_scope": "paragraph",
                  "manual_review_required": true
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "normalized_workflow": {
                "text": "Correctional institutions also report a high number of individuals living with mental illness.",
                "level": 0,
                "list_kind": "number",
                "list_marker_hint": ""
              },
              "warning_entries": [
                {
                  "warning_code": "NUMBERED_SEQUENCE_REVIEW",
                  "warning_message": "Numbering intent was observed, but ordered sequence should be reviewed in the DOCX.",
                  "warning_scope": "paragraph",
                  "manual_review_required": true
                }
              ]
            }
          ]
        },
        {

            "placeholder_type": null,

          },
